metadata.title: DAO & Decentralized Organizations Knowledge Graph
metadata.description: Concept dependency graph for DAO and decentralized organizations — 18 nodes, 22 edges
metadata.source: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Lecture Series / Vitalik Buterin, Taylor Pearson, Aragon, Nick Tomaino, Jesse Walden

nodes.name: Blockchain Fundamentals
nodes.description: Learn the basic principles of blockchain, consensus mechanisms (PoW/PoS), smart contracts, and the difference between coins and tokens.
key_ideas: Blockchain = A tamper-proof ledger distributed globally
PoW (Proof of Work) vs PoS (Proof of Stake) consensus mechanisms
Smart contracts: Code that executes automatically when conditions are met
Coins (own chain) vs Tokens (issued on other chains)
nodes.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Lecture

nodes.name: Definition and Structure of DAOs
nodes.description: Learn the definition of Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs), differences from traditional corporations, and token-based governance structures.
key_ideas: DAO = Organization with operating rules recorded on blockchain as smart contracts
Traditional corporation: Board decides → Employees execute
DAO: Token holders vote → Smart contract auto-executes
Operates automatically without intermediary managers
nodes.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Lecture

nodes.name: TheDAO Hack and Lessons Learned
nodes.description: Learn about the 2016 TheDAO hack, re-entrancy attack, and Ethereum hard fork decision.
key_ideas: 2016: Slock.it launched TheDAO on Ethereum → $150M raised
Re-entrancy exploit drained 30% of funds
Ethereum community decided to hard fork (block 1,920,000)
Lesson: Importance of smart contract security audits
nodes.paper_ref: Aragon Blog, '15 Ways the World is Being Transformed by DAOs' (2021)

nodes.name: Convex vs Concave Decision-Making
nodes.description: Learn Vitalik Buterin's Convex/Concave decision-making framework. Understand when centralization vs decentralization is more effective.
key_ideas: Convex: Extreme choice is best (military strategy, rapid product decisions)
Concave: Middle ground/compromise is best (fund allocation, policy decisions)
Convex problems → Centralized few should handle
Concave problems → Entire community should decide

nodes.name: DAO vs Corporation Debate
nodes.description: Learn the criticism that DAOs will eventually converge to corporate structures and Vitalik's counter-argument.
key_ideas: Corporation camp: Hierarchy makes decisions efficient, DAOs will converge
Vitalik: DAOs are closer to Sovereigns, not Corporations
DAOs should learn from political science, not business management
Succession Problem is the key challenge

nodes.name: Pod Structure and Hybrid Governance
nodes.description: Learn about the Pod structure combining decentralization and centralization through the Ukraine DAO case study.
key_ideas: Fundraising: Decentralized approach
Fund execution: Centralized within Pods after allocation
Upper level (Core): Focus on Concave situations
Lower level (Pod): Focus on Convex situations

nodes.name: Censorship Resistance and Decentralization
nodes.description: Learn the principles of how decentralization provides censorship resistance and cases like Pirate Bay, Sci-Hub, and BitTorrent.
key_ideas: Pirate Bay, Sci-Hub: Only domain managers face legal risk, data stays decentralized
BitTorrent: Anyone can join/leave without affecting file availability
DAO's censorship resistance: Goal is stable maintenance, not permanent evasion
Maintain organization stability under uncertainty and interference

nodes.name: Kleros Decentralized Court Case Study
nodes.description: Learn about 'trustworthy fairness' as decentralization through the Kleros decentralized dispute resolution platform.
key_ideas: Random selection of 3,000 jurors from PNK token stakers
Jurors don't know each other, vote after 3-day evidence review
Automatic compensation payment via smart contract
Limitation: Case #1170 controversy over insider token mobilization

nodes.name: Organization Man and Social Ethic
nodes.description: Learn William Whyte's Organization Man concept and the background of 20th century corporate-centric society formation.
key_ideas: Organization Man: Named by William Whyte in 1956
Shift from individualism → collectivism (Social Ethic)
Belief that 'belongingness' is the ultimate human need
Rational adaptation to 20th century corporate-dominated society

nodes.name: Blockchain Man and the New Paradigm
nodes.description: Learn Taylor Pearson's Blockchain Man concept and how reduced transaction costs change organization size.
key_ideas: If internet lowered transaction costs by one order of magnitude, blockchain will lower it by another
As transaction costs approach zero, optimal firm size approaches one person
Frontier scientist-type humans are the prototype of Blockchain Man
Hollywood model: Project-based collaboration becomes the norm

nodes.name: Fat Protocol Thesis
nodes.description: Learn the Fat Protocol thesis that explains the difference in value distribution structure between internet and blockchain.
key_ideas: Internet: Thin protocol (free) + Fat application (Google, Facebook)
Blockchain: Fat protocol (ETH, SOL) + Thin application
Blockchain protocols have built-in tokens that directly accumulate value
Current debate: DeFi/NFT applications also create massive value

nodes.name: 15 Application Areas of DAOs
nodes.description: Learn about the various areas being transformed by DAOs: developer collectives, worker collectives, art collectives, asset management, etc.
key_ideas: Developer Collectives: BadgerDAO, Gitcoin, API3
Worker Collectives: Raid Guild, dOrg — Modern revival of medieval guilds
Art Collectives: PleasrDAO — Holds iconic NFTs in crypto history
Metaverse: Decentraland — Fully decentralized metaverse
nodes.paper_ref: Aragon Blog, '15 Ways the World is Being Transformed by DAOs' (2021)

nodes.name: Why Firms Exist
nodes.description: Learn Ronald Coase's transaction cost theory and the economic reasons for the existence of firms.
key_ideas: Transaction cost minimization — Ronald Coase, 'The Nature of the Firm' (1937)
Aggregation of capital and talent — Vitalari & Shaughnessy
Provides stability and job security to risk-averse individuals
Most people dislike corporations but need them
nodes.paper_ref: Nick Tomaino, 'The Slow Death of the Firm' (2017)

nodes.name: Bitcoin Thriving Without a Firm
nodes.description: Learn how Bitcoin achieved a sustainable digital service without a legal entity.
key_ideas: Miners: Performance measured by PoW, directly rewarded in BTC by protocol
Developers/Users: Hold BTC → Indirectly rewarded through value appreciation
2017: Bitcoin market cap $100B > Goldman Sachs market cap
2026: Bitcoin market cap ~$1.7T, tens of times larger than Goldman Sachs
nodes.paper_ref: Nick Tomaino, 'The Slow Death of the Firm' (2017)

nodes.name: Key Barriers for Decentralized Organizations
nodes.description: Learn about the difficulties of decentralized decision-making, contribution measurement problems, and misuse of 'decentralization'.
key_ideas: Decentralized decision-making is hard — No governance best practices yet
Most labor types are difficult to accurately measure contribution
Misuse and ambiguity of 'decentralization'
Some centralized decision-making is needed initially
nodes.paper_ref: Nick Tomaino, 'The Slow Death of the Firm' (2017)

nodes.name: Progressive Decentralization Strategy
nodes.description: Learn a16z's progressive decentralization playbook — PMF → Community Participation → Sufficient Decentralization sequence.
key_ideas: Stage 1: Product/Market Fit — Core team leads all decisions
Stage 2: Community Participation — Strengthen community harmony
Stage 3: Sufficient Decentralization — Broad token distribution
Failure pattern: Pursuing community ownership first only attracts speculators

nodes.name: Howey Test and Securities Classification
nodes.description: Learn about the Howey Test, the criteria for classifying crypto tokens as securities.
key_ideas: 4 requirements: Investment, common enterprise, profit expectation, reliance on others' efforts
Meeting all four → Likely classified as security
Sufficient decentralization → No reliance on others' efforts → Non-security
Cases: Kik $5M fine, Ripple partial win, Ethereum recognized as non-security

nodes.name: Exit to Community
nodes.description: Learn about the exit-to-community strategy where teams transfer ownership to users and the cooperative economic model.
key_ideas: Core team transfers majority ownership (fees, control)
Token can transition to non-security status
User-owners realize increasing returns through cooperative economic model
Success cases: Uniswap (UNI), Compound (COMP), Optimism (OP), Arbitrum (ARB)

edges.description: Understanding blockchain basics is a prerequisite for learning DAO definition
Need to know DAO definition to understand TheDAO incident context
Understanding DAO structure enables learning decision-making framework
Convex/Concave concept is the foundation for DAO vs Corporation debate
Decision-making framework is the basis for Pod structure design
DAO's decentralized nature is the foundation for censorship resistance
Censorship resistance concept provides background for Kleros case analysis
DAO is an alternative organizational form to Organization Man era
Evolution from Organization Man to Blockchain Man
Blockchain Man concept connects to Fat Protocol thesis
Understanding DAO definition enables learning various application areas
Blockchain understanding enables re-examination of why firms exist
Need to know why firms exist to understand Bitcoin's innovation
Bitcoin case enables discussion of decentralized org barriers
Understanding barriers motivates progressive decentralization strategy
Progressive decentralization connects to Howey Test compliance strategy
Understanding Howey Test provides rationale for Exit to Community
TheDAO incident is a historical case showing decentralized org barriers
Kleros case is a practical example of progressive decentralization
Various DAO types are real cases of Exit to Community
Understanding value distribution influences decentralization strategy design
DAO vs Corporation debate contributes to understanding decentralized org barriers
